text: Hello my friends. Welcome back to
English Unleashed. I'm Tom. Today we're
going to talk about something that
almost every English learner struggles
with. It's listening. Not just any
listening, real listening. The kind of
listening where you understand English
without subtitles. Now, maybe you watch
films in English. Maybe you enjoy TV
shows or YouTube videos. And maybe you
always put on subtitles because you are
afraid you will not understand.
Subtitles help you. They feel safe. But
there is a problem. When you read
subtitles, your eyes do the work, not
your ears.
You think you are listening but really
you are reading and then when you turn
the subtitles off you feel lost. You say
I don't understand anything.
This is why many learners never improve
their listening. They depend on
subtitles.
Subtitles become like a crutch,
something you cannot walk without.
Today, I want to show you a new way, a
better way, a way to train your ears and
your brain so you can understand English
naturally without reading.
So let me ask you what if I told you
that there is a secret
a simple secret that most learners miss.
It is not about memorizing more words.
It is not about listening for 10 hours a
day. It is about how you listen. It is
about training your brain step by step
until English sounds clear, until
English feels natural. This is what we
will explore together.
All right. The first step is to choose
the right kind of material.
This sounds simple, but many learners
make a mistake here. They choose
something that is far too difficult.
They turn on the news in English. The
news is very fast.
The speakers talk quickly or learners
choose a complicated movie, a difficult
movie. The actors speak fast. They use
slang. They use jokes. And sometimes the
sound is not clear.
When learners listen to these things,
they often feel sad. They feel
disappointed.
They say, "My English is bad. I cannot
understand anything." But let me tell
you something. This is not your fault.
It is not because you are bad at
English. It is not because you cannot
learn. It is simply the wrong choice.
The problem is not you. The problem is
the material.
If you try to climb a very high mountain
on your first day, of course you will
fall. But if you start with a small
hill, you can walk, you can climb, and
you can succeed. Listening is the same.
At the beginning, you need what I call
high context English. Okay, what does
this mean? Well, high context means that
you can understand the situation, the
action and the meaning. Even when you do
not understand every single word, you do
not feel lost. You can guess what is
happening. Let me give you an example.
Imagine you are watching a children's
cartoon in English. In the cartoon, the
characters move a lot. The pictures show
you the story.
A child wakes up, washes his face, eats
breakfast, and goes to school. The
pictures help you understand. Even if
you do not catch all the words, you see
the action, you hear the words, and your
brain connects them together so you
understand. Another example is a simple
story podcast for learners.
The narrator speaks slowly. The
sentences are short. The story is about
daily life. I brush my teeth. I eat
breakfast. I go to school. These are
actions you already know. These are
routines you live every day.
Because you know them, your brain can
guess the meaning even when you do not
hear every sound clearly. This is high
context listening. High context
listening is powerful because it gives
you confidence. You do not feel like you
are drowning in the middle of a big
ocean of English words. You feel like
you are standing in shallow water. The
water is safe. You can swim a little.
You can move. You can practice.
And when you feel stronger, you can go
deeper.
It is the same with listening. At the
beginning, choose simple high context
material. A cartoon, a story, a short
podcast, something clear, something
predictable.
When you do this, your brain learns to
connect sound with meaning. Your ears
become stronger and day by day, little
by little, you will be ready for more
difficult listening. Okay, let's move to
the next step. This step is about how
you listen.
Many learners press play, listen once
and then stop. They say, "I don't
understand." They feel frustrated and
they give up. But listening only once is
not enough. Your brain needs more time.
Your ears need more training.
That's why I use something I call the
three listen rule. Okay, the three
listen rule.
The three listen rule is very simple but
it's very powerful. It means that you
listen to the same thing three times.
Not once, not twice, three times.
And each time you listen, you have a
different goal. The first lesson is for
gist.
Gist means the big picture, the overall
meaning. You're not trying to understand
every single word. You're not trying to
catch every sound. You are only asking
yourself, "What is this about?"
That's all. For example, you listen to a
short story. You may not understand all
the words. But you understand that it is
about travel or maybe it is about food
or maybe it is about a family at home.
That's enough. You got the gist. You got
the big picture.
The second lesson is different. Now you
listen for key phrases.
A key phrase is a group of words that
gives meaning.
A key phrase is often repeated.
A key phrase is often important in the
story. For example, you may hear the
phrase, "I woke up early."
You may hear, "I went to the shop." You
may hear, "I felt tired."
When you listen the second time, you
begin to notice these phrases.
They start to sound familiar.
You begin to remember how they sound
together, not just as a single word. The
third listen is for details.
Details are the small pieces of
information.
Maybe you hear that the person woke up
Maybe you hear that they bought three
apples.
Maybe you hear that it was raining.
These are details.
When you listen the third time, you do
not worry if you miss some words, but
you try to notice a few of these
details.
They make the story more clear. They
make the meaning richer. So you see the
three listen rule is like looking at a
picture. The first time you stand far
away, you see the big shape. The second
time you come closer, you see the lines,
the patterns.
The third time you stand very close. You
see the small colors, the small objects.
Together, all three views give you a
full picture.
It is the same with listening.
The first listen gives you the gist. The
second lesson gives you the phrases. The
third lesson gives you the details.
Okay. Now, let's talk about something
that makes many learners worried. It is
the idea that they must understand every
single word. Many learners think if I
don't understand all the words then I
understand nothing. They panic when they
miss a word. They stop listening. They
feel like a failure. But let me tell you
a secret. Even native speakers do not
understand every word all the time.
Yeah, that is true. Imagine two native
speakers are talking and one person uses
a new word, maybe a difficult technical
word. The other person does not know it.
But do they stop and say, "Oh no, I
cannot understand English." They just
keep listening. They focus on the
meaning. They understand from the other
words, from the situation, from the
context, what that word means. This is
what you must do too. Real listening is
not about catching 100% of the words.
Real listening is about catching the
message. It is about understanding the
story even if a few words are missing.
Let me give you an example. Imagine a
friend says, "Hey, do you want to grab a
coffee later?"
Maybe you don't understand the word
grab, right? That's okay. You hear
coffee. You hear later. You hear the
question sound in their voice and you
understand this person wants to drink
coffee with me. That is the meaning and
that's enough. This is how listening
works in real life. You pick up the
important words. You connect them to the
situation. Your brain feels the gaps and
step by step your understanding grows.
So tell yourself this, I do not need
every word. I need the message. This is
the way to build real listening
confidence. So now let's put all of this
into action. How can you practice every
day? First, choose something simple. Do
not choose a long, difficult movie.
Choose a short podcast. Choose a simple
video, something with a clear topic.
Second, listen without subtitles. This
is the most important part. Do not let
your eyes do the work. Trust your ears.
Use the three listen rule. First, listen
for the gist, the big idea. Then listen
again for phrases, the expressions that
repeat. Then listen one more time for
details, the small pieces of
information. Here you can use subtitles.
Okay, you can check the words and
expressions you didn't catch. So, repeat
this every day. Even 10 minutes is
enough. The secret is not listening once
a week. The secret is daily practice.
Every day, your brain learns a little
more. Every day, your ears get stronger.
Thank you so much for joining me today
on English Unleashed. I'm Tom and until
next time, keep listening, keep
practicing, and keep believing in
yourself. Bye for now.
